title: The man who collected broken umbrellas
summary: A small portrait of a man who picks up broken umbrellas after storms and gives them new names.
body:
There is a man on my street who collects broken umbrellas. I noticed him for the first time about two years ago, on a windy March morning. The night before, a storm had passed through the city, and the pavements were full of the dead: black umbrellas with bent ribs, cheap pink ones turned inside out, and one large blue one that looked, from a distance, like a small wrecked ship.

Most people walked around them. He did not. He bent down, picked them up, looked at each one carefully, and put it under his arm. By the end of the street, he was carrying eight or nine.

I was curious. One day I greeted him and asked, politely, what he did with them. He smiled. 'I take them home,' he said. 'I open them up. I see if the cloth is still good. If the ribs can be bent back, I bend them. If not, I keep the handle for another umbrella.'

'And then what?' I asked.

'Then I give them away,' he said. 'I leave them in the entrances of buildings on rainy days. Or by the bus stop. I don't put a note. I don't watch to see who takes them.'

I thought about this for a long time afterwards. I had assumed he was poor, or strange, or both. In fact he was a retired engineer with a small pension and an obvious mind. He did not need to do this.

When I asked him why, he answered slowly. 'When I was a child,' he said, 'we did not throw broken things away. We fixed them. I was good at fixing. The world is now very rich in broken things and very poor in people who fix them. And an umbrella that has lost only one rib still has six good ones. That is a lot of usefulness, lying on the street.'

I think of him often, now, when something of mine breaks. I am not very good at fixing things. But I try harder to ask the question first: is this really finished, or only tired?

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect with past simple
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Sự kết hợp: dùng 'past perfect' (had + V3) cho điều xảy ra trước một mốc quá khứ: 'The night before, a storm had passed through the city.' (Trước buổi sáng đó, cơn bão đã đi qua.)
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the past perfect for an action before another past action: 'The night before, a storm had passed through the city, and the pavements were full of the dead.'

blanks.explanation: A storm had passed through the city.
He keeps the handle for another umbrella.
'We did not throw broken things away.'
If one rib is lost, six good ones remain (umbrellas usually have 7-8 ribs).
'Is this really finished, or only tired?'

translationVi:
Trên con phố nhà tôi có một người chuyên đi nhặt những chiếc ô gãy. Tôi để ý đến ông lần đầu khoảng hai năm trước, vào một buổi sáng tháng Ba nhiều gió. Đêm hôm trước, một cơn bão đi qua thành phố, vỉa hè đầy 'xác' ô: ô đen với nan cong, những chiếc ô hồng rẻ tiền bị lật ngược, và một chiếc ô xanh to mà nhìn từ xa trông như một con thuyền nhỏ bị đắm.

Phần lớn người ta đi vòng qua chúng. Ông thì không. Ông cúi xuống, nhặt lên, ngắm kỹ từng chiếc, rồi kẹp dưới nách. Đi hết con phố, ông đã ôm tám chín chiếc.

Tôi tò mò. Một hôm tôi chào ông và hỏi lịch sự rằng ông làm gì với chúng. Ông mỉm cười. 'Tôi mang về nhà,' ông nói. 'Tôi mở ra. Xem vải còn dùng được không. Nan nào còn nắn lại được, tôi nắn. Nếu không, tôi giữ tay cầm cho ô khác.'

'Rồi sao nữa ạ?' tôi hỏi.

'Rồi tôi cho đi,' ông đáp. 'Tôi để chúng ở cửa các tòa nhà vào ngày mưa. Hoặc cạnh trạm xe buýt. Tôi không để lại lời nhắn. Tôi cũng không đứng nhìn ai lấy.'

Tôi đã nghĩ về việc này rất lâu sau đó. Tôi từng đoán ông nghèo, hay lạ lùng, hoặc cả hai. Thực ra ông là một kỹ sư đã nghỉ hưu, có lương hưu nhỏ và đầu óc tỉnh táo. Ông không cần phải làm việc này.

Khi tôi hỏi vì sao, ông trả lời chậm rãi. 'Khi tôi còn bé,' ông nói, 'chúng tôi không vứt đồ hỏng. Chúng tôi sửa. Tôi sửa giỏi. Thế giới bây giờ rất giàu đồ hỏng và rất nghèo người sửa chúng. Một chiếc ô gãy chỉ một nan thì vẫn còn sáu nan tốt. Đó là rất nhiều sự hữu ích, đang nằm trên đường.'

Giờ tôi hay nghĩ về ông, mỗi khi vật gì của tôi bị hỏng. Tôi không khéo tay. Nhưng tôi cố gắng hỏi câu hỏi đầu tiên: thứ này thật sự đã hỏng, hay chỉ mệt thôi?

questions.prompt: When did the writer first notice the man?
options: About two years ago, on a windy morning in March
questions.explanation: 'I noticed him for the first time about two years ago, on a windy March morning.'

questions.prompt: What had happened the night before?
questions.explanation: 'The night before, a storm had passed through the city.'

questions.prompt: What does the man do with the broken umbrellas?
options: Repairs the ones he can and gives them away on rainy days
questions.explanation: He repairs them and leaves them in building entrances.
questions.hint: Look for what he does after he fixes them.

questions.prompt: Where does he leave the umbrellas?
options: In the entrances of buildings and by bus stops
questions.explanation: 'I leave them in the entrances of buildings on rainy days. Or by the bus stop.'

questions.prompt: What did the writer first assume about the man?
options: He was poor or strange, or both
questions.explanation: 'I had assumed he was poor, or strange, or both.'

options: A retired engineer with a small pension
questions.explanation: 'He was a retired engineer with a small pension and an obvious mind.'

questions.prompt: What did people do when he was a child?
options: Fix broken things instead of throwing them away
questions.explanation: 'When I was a child, we did not throw broken things away. We fixed them.'

questions.prompt: How does he describe the modern world?
options: Rich in broken things and poor in people who fix them
questions.explanation: 'The world is now very rich in broken things and very poor in people who fix them.'

questions.prompt: What does the writer try to do now?
options: Ask whether something is really finished or only tired
questions.explanation: 'I try harder to ask the question first: is this really finished, or only tired?'

options: Many objects we call 'broken' still have life in them, and so might other things
questions.explanation: The umbrella with one bad rib but six good ones is the central image.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The man asks whether something is 'really finished, or only tired'. Could the same question apply to friendships, careers, or habits — and how would you tell the difference?
